summary: This advice column features Cathy O'Neil's 'Aunt Pythia' persona responding to reader questions about sex, relationships, academic philosophy's toxic culture, and maintaining idealism in a corrupt world. The most substantive response addresses the Colin McGinn sexual harassment case, using it to critique philosophy's culture of bullying and lack of external accountability compared to math and science. O'Neil argues that philosophy's reliance on rhetorical cleverness rather than evidence or logical proof enables bad actors to rationalize predatory behavior. The column balances irreverent humor with genuine ethical reflection on personal autonomy and institutional accountability.
key_points: Philosophy's lack of external verification (unlike math's proofs or science's experiments) creates a culture where rhetorical cleverness can excuse bad behavior
Women's underrepresentation in philosophy is linked to both bullying culture and sexual predation, and the field needs to actively address this
Being your own moral authority is essential - don't outsource ethical decisions to others or arbitrary rules

Maintaining idealism requires playing the long game: have clear personal standards, avoid burning out by trying to fix everything, and don't pin your ideals on individual heroes who may disappoint you
If you feel complicit in something evil, leave - even if it means quitting your job
money_quotes: I like math because it's filled with people who know how to admit they're wrong.
I don't like philosophy because arguments are slippery.
My real advice is to be your own moral authority next time. Your life, go live it.
Once you've been politicized and sensitized to the evil that organizations do or are involved in, you start to see it everywhere.
If you feel complicit in something you consider evil, then get the fuck out, even if it means quitting your job.
Don't depend on a single person or a couple of persons to embody the ideals that you care about, because they'll probably end up disappointing you.
Write up an internal list of your ideals, they'll never let you down.
I'm not saying the field can't recover, but until they work on it, I won't feel sorry for the fact that women are under-represented.
key_insight: Fields without external accountability mechanisms - whether empirical evidence or mathematical proof - are more vulnerable to cultures of bullying and abuse because rhetorical cleverness can always manufacture justifications for bad behavior.
